text:
PARIS 芒聙聰 The roars were deafening, rattling around La D脙漏fense Arena, pressing down on eardrums, vibrating the air. The screaming voices were urging the man of the hour, the day, the Olympiad, to go faster in pursuit of glory.

芒聙聹It sounded like a soccer stadium,芒聙聺 said Carson Foster, an American tasked with trying to chase the man of the hour, the day, the Olympiad.

This wasn芒聙聶t a soccer stadium; it was a swim meet, and it was the loudest four minutes I芒聙聶ve ever heard in 32 years of covering the sport. It was a Frenchman in France, enjoying the biggest Olympic home-pool advantage since Australian Ian Thorpe 芒聙聹Thorpedo芒聙聺 in Sydney in 2000. And L脙漏on Marchand met the moment.

Every time Marchand rose above the water芒聙聶s surface to take a breath during the breastroke leg of the 400-meter individual medley, some 14,000 fans bellowed at him in unison. It was a rhythmic exhortation, pushing the 22-year-old from Toulouse further and further ahead in the third leg of the race, until it wasn芒聙聶t even a race anymore.脗

All that remained over the final 100 meters was a coronation, and the pursuit of his own history. Marchand芒聙聶s time of 4:02.95 took down Michael Phelps芒聙聶 Olympic record in the most difficult event, but did not quite break his own world record of 4:02.50. (We appear to have the dreaded Slow Pool on our hands at these Olympics.)脗

芒聙聹My objective was to break a record tonight,芒聙聺 Marchand said. 芒聙聹I felt really good. In the last [lap], I took advantage of the crowd because I knew I was ahead, I knew I was an Olympic champion.芒聙聺

That narrow miss of the record he set last year hardly mattered at the end. Marchand芒聙聶s gold-medal rout of silver medalist Tomoyuki Matsushita of Japan and bronze medalist Foster delivered on expectations that were set Eiffel Tower-high last year, when he first erased Phelps芒聙聶 400 IM world record. Coached by Bob Bowman, the same man who mentored Phelps to a record 28 Olympic medals (23 of them gold), Marchand is now officially the biggest star of the Paris Olympics.

The son of two French Olympic swimmers had toleave the country to maximize his potential, inviting himself to swim for Bowman at Arizona State with a cold-call email. But France fully embraced its expatriate aquaman for these games, elevating him to a Victor Wembanyama or Kylian Mbapp脙漏 level of celebrity.

芒聙聹I felt really proud to be myself, and to be also French,芒聙聺 Marchand said. 芒聙聹It was an amazing time for me, and I was really living in the moment.芒聙聺

The evening began with a festival atmosphere outside the area. French fans were chanting, singing and drinking outside La D脙漏fense Arena, waiting for the doors to open. They wore masks emblazoned with Marchand芒聙聶s face. Or they carried cutout images of him. One little boy wore his Arizona State swim cap.

芒聙聹Al-lez! L脙漏-on! Al-lez! L脙漏-on! Al-lez, L脙漏-on!芒聙聺 they chanted.

And then they went inside and made more noise. The entire arena sang a French song in unison 12 minutes before the race. They cheered over the top of Deep Purple芒聙聶s 芒聙聹Smoke on the Water,芒聙聺 then they watched Marchand smoke the water.

They were on their feet and bellowing from the moment Marchand was introduced until after he touched the wall. When the Frenchman straddled the lane line and thrust his hands in the air, they exulted. When he blew them a kiss, they swooned. When he leaped from the pool and placed both hands on his hips, a conqueror looking down at his aquatic kingdom, they roared.

Then they sang the French national anthem with Marchand, who briefly allowed his eyelids to flutter closed while on the podium. He芒聙聶s a perfect swimming machine芒聙聰quite literally made for this moment芒聙聰but he芒聙聶s a human being who was savoring the perfect confluence of time and place and accomplishment.

Bob Bowman isn芒聙聶t one to get caught up in technical terms, so he describes the swimming study as 芒聙聹how your boat floats.芒聙聺 In more precise language, part of theNeptune Project in Francewas creating a hydrodynamic profile of its elite swimmers芒聙聰essentially measuring how easily they go through the water.

One swimmer measured off the charts in the yearlong study: L脙漏on Marchand.

芒聙聹He has the best results I芒聙聶ve ever seen,芒聙聺 says Ricardo Peterson, one of the data analysts who worked on the project. 芒聙聹By far.芒聙聺

Simply put, Marchand is built to slice through a pool like nobody else. Swimmers have long talked about a vague-but-real 芒聙聹feel for the water,芒聙聺 and the French study may actually have quantified it.

Peterson and his colleagues identified 芒聙聹passive and active drag芒聙聺 affecting swimmers. Passive drag measured them in the gliding position primarily on starts and turns芒聙聰when they are streamlined and not actively moving their limbs. Active drag measured them during actual swimming movements. Marchand was elite in both categories.脗

芒聙聹He芒聙聶s almost like natural selection,芒聙聺 Peterson says of his body type. Legs create more drag than torso because they don芒聙聶t float, and Peterson says Marchand is blessed with a larger and longer torso and shorter legs芒聙聰much like Michael Phelps. Via nature or nurture, he also has an optimal swimming posture.

芒聙聹He has a uniform body, in the streamline position, from shoulders to feet,芒聙聺 Peterson observes. 芒聙聹He芒聙聶s an outlier in terms of morphological characteristics.芒聙聺

When not gliding, Marchand is both ferocity and poetry in motion. His stroke technique is impeccable, and his underwater work might be the best in the world at this point. In the crucial 15 meters at the starts and turns when swimmers are allowed to be submerged, Marchand blows away his competition. There are greater drag forces on the surface than beneath it, so the work down below is critical.

芒聙聹L脙漏on is much, much better than anyone else underwater,芒聙聺 Peterson says. 芒聙聹His movement underwater is very symmetric, and he芒聙聶s able to sustain his underwaters for a very long time. He can reduce drag by staying underwater. Comparable to other swimmers, he has a lower energy cost.芒聙聺

That helps explain how he can do what he does without overwhelming physical prowess. Marchand on dry land is an unremarkable sight; he芒聙聶s about 6'2" and not overly muscled. His handshake is as fluid as water芒聙聰which, come to think of it, might be telling.

Some of this may simply come down to good genes. Marchand is the son of two French Olympic swimmers, Xavier and C脙漏line, which certainly helps account for body type and 芒聙聹feel.芒聙聺 But the other element that has to be present in addition to physical gifts is work ethic. Marchand has that in abundance.

After selling himself to Bowman in a cold-call email, Marchand showed up at Arizona State as an accomplished swimmer but not a superstar芒聙聰he芒聙聶d competed in the Tokyo Olympics but wasn芒聙聶t a medal threat. Nobody could have easily foreseen what he芒聙聶s become. It芒聙聶s the work he put in at the Mona Plummer Aquatic Center at ASU, under the tutelage of arguably the greatest IM coach of all time, that turned him the sensation he is today at age 22.

芒聙聹He just pushes himself to the limit, and past the limit a lot of times,芒聙聺 says Hubie Kos, who swam with Marchand at Arizona State and will continue to do so at Texas, where Bowman recently moved. 芒聙聹I trained with a lot of people who don芒聙聶t show the same kind of potential in practice that they do in meets, but L脙漏on is quite the opposite. He has crazy practices.芒聙聺

Adds another ASU swimmer, Grant House: 芒聙聹Everybody breaks down at our practices eventually, and I芒聙聶ve only seen him break down about three times in three years. When he does it芒聙聶s like, 芒聙聵Oh my gosh, he芒聙聶s human after all.芒聙聶芒聙聺

L脙漏on Marchand was standing underneath a maze of scaffolding underneath the stands in the arena when someone handed him a phone. He pressed it to his right ear. On the line: French president Emmanuel Macron.

芒聙聹That was a first for me,芒聙聺 Marchand said. 芒聙聹He called me and told me that he was watching the final with all his family and everyone was kind of screaming on the phone.芒聙聺

It might not be his last presidential call of the coming week. Marchand still has up to three races ahead: the 200 IM, 200 breaststroke and 200 butterfly. The 400 IM is his unbeatable event, but he will have a chance to win all the others, and certainly will be a medal threat in each.

The challenge now will be returning to earth and returning to the grind of this meet. Marchand has Monday off, but will dive back in with both the 200 fly and 200 breaststroke preliminaries and semifinals on Tuesday芒聙聰a killer double, but one he has been planning to take on for a while now. The final in those events will be Wednesday, and then the 200 IM rounds begin on Thursday.

If anyone has been coached for this multitasking, it芒聙聶s Marchand. Bowman guided Phelps through incredible workloads and understands the other elements that come into play芒聙聰all the press conferences, the drug testing, the warmups and warmdowns, and recovery work. Managing everything outside the swimming is where the Olympics becomes a real beast.

Marchand was sufficiently ready for this moment that Bowman didn芒聙聶t have to do much pre-race coaching. There was nothing to discuss. If there was a message to convey, it could be boiled down to:Go be L脙漏on.

芒聙聹I don芒聙聶t think we had to talk about the race,芒聙聺 Marchand said. 芒聙聹We were both ready to do what we had to do. I think before the race he was telling me to relax and stay happy. This was a pretty good chance for me to be home at the Olympics.芒聙聺

It芒聙聶s been a long time since a swimmer enjoyed this kind of home-pool advantage, if ever. France芒聙聶s L脙漏on Love-In was an audio bombardment that will echo through the rest of these Olympics.

Pat Forde is a senior writer for Sports Illustrated who covers college football and college basketball as well as the Olympics and horse racing. He cohosts the College Football Enquirer podcast and is a football analyst on the Big Ten Network. He previously worked for Yahoo Sports, ESPN and The (Louisville) Courier-Journal. Forde has won 28 Associated Press Sports Editors writing contest awards, has been published three times in the Best American Sports Writing book series, and was nominated for the 1990 Pulitzer Prize. A past president of the U.S. Basketball Writers Association and member of the Football Writers Association of America, he lives in Louisville with his wife. They have three children, all of whom were collegiate swimmers.

title: L脙漏on Marchand Captured the 400 IM Olympic Gold and the Hearts of the French People
description: The biggest star of the Paris Games took down Michael Phelps芒聙聶 Olympic record in the most difficult event, putting his uncanny physical gifts and work ethic on display in front of a roaring hometown crowd.脗聽
